CARVEYANCE History · The Off-World File

The Apollo Lunar Roving Vehicle

$38 Million
and One
Horsepower

The most expensive car ever built made roughly one horsepower, wore tyres woven from piano wire, was designed by a penniless refugee, folded into a spaceship, and is still parked exactly where we left it. This is the Moon Buggy.

By CarveyanceHistory2026

Humans are old. Depending on where you draw the line, we have been recognisably ourselves for two or three hundred thousand years. In that time we worked out fire. The wheel. Agriculture, medicine, written language, the movement of the planets, the length of the year to the day. We built the pyramids and Stonehenge and the aqueducts. We mapped the human body, reasoned our way to gravity, invented gunpowder, sailed to every continent on Earth.

And in all that time — hundreds of thousands of years — we could not fly.

Leonardo da Vinci sketched flying machines in the fifteenth century and none of them worked. For four hundred more years, nothing. Then two brothers who fixed and sold bicycles in Dayton, Ohio, took a wood-and-fabric contraption to a windy beach in North Carolina, and on 17 December 1903 they flew it. Twelve seconds. A hundred and twenty feet. Less than the wingspan of the airliner you will one day be bored inside of.

Sixty-eight years after that beach, a man named David Scott sat down in a car, on the Moon, and drove it.

Sit with that number. Not sixty-eight centuries. Sixty-eight years. One long human lifetime separates the first powered hop off a sand dune from a battery-electric four-wheel-drive vehicle motoring across the Sea of Rains at the bottom of the Apennine mountains, a quarter of a million miles from the nearest repair shop.

People will tell you the great leap of the twentieth century was medicine, or computing, and those are wonderful things. But we have been making medicine since we chewed willow bark for headaches; the story there is one of steady, glorious accumulation. Flight to spaceflight was not accumulation. It was a vertical wall, climbed in a single lifetime, by people who decided it would be done and were — for one brief, expensive, extraordinary window — not slowed down by anyone telling them it couldn't.

And because we are the most incurably automotive species that has ever existed, once we got there — once we had actually landed on another world — we did the only thing that made any sense.

We brought a car.

Is there anything more human than that? We crossed the void, we planted the flag, and then we decided the walking was tedious and unpacked a convertible. This is the story of that car: the most expensive single vehicle ever built, powered by a total of roughly one horsepower, with tyres woven from piano wire, that is still — right now, tonight — parked exactly where we left it.

First, Three Corrections

It was not a golf cart, and it did not ride in the Space Shuttle.

The Moon Buggy was officially the Apollo Lunar Roving Vehicle, the LRV, and it was a spacecraft that happened to have wheels — held to the same "no single failure shall abort the mission" standard as the Command Module and the lunar lander themselves.

The Space Shuttle came a decade later and has nothing to do with this. The rover travelled to the Moon folded into the side of the Apollo Lunar Module's descent stage, in a wedge-shaped bay called Quadrant 1, and was unfolded by hand after landing.

And it was not, whatever else you have read, the first wheels on the Moon. We will get to who beat them.

Why Build It At All

The first moonwalkers were prisoners of their own boots.

The early Apollo landings were miracles, but they were miracles on foot. Armstrong and Aldrin, on Apollo 11, never got more than about sixty-five yards from their lander. Everything they did would have fit inside a football field. The suits were bulky, the oxygen was finite, and there was an iron rule: you could never go farther out than you could walk back if something failed.

For the final three missions — Apollo 15, 16 and 17, the so-called "J missions" — NASA wanted real geology. Mountains. Crater rims. Ancient lava channels. Places miles from the safety of the lander. You cannot do that on foot with a life-support backpack and a countdown running.

The rover changed the entire proposition. On the first of their three drives, Scott and Irwin covered more ground than Apollo 11, 12 and 14 had managed combined. As the Apollo 15 launch director Rocco Petrone put it afterward, in four words: It made the mission.

Who Built It: Boeing, GM, and a Hungarian Refugee

NASA's Marshall Space Flight Center ran the program. Boeing was the prime contractor. And the company that made it actually behave like a vehicle — the wheels, the motors, the suspension, the steering — was General Motors, through its Defense Research Laboratories in Santa Barbara.

This is not a footnote. Strip away the chassis and the electronics, and the parts that let a machine drive on alien soil were GM's. The program was led on the GM side by Sam Romano and a man named Ferenc Pavlics, and Pavlics's story deserves telling in full.

Ferenc Pavlics arrived in America in 1957 — the year the Soviets launched Sputnik — as a penniless Hungarian refugee.

He went to work on the problem of how a wheel could function on a surface nobody had ever touched, and he arrived at an answer so elegant that NASA later gave him special recognition for inventing what it called the "resilient wheel." A refugee who came to the United States with nothing designed the tyres that drove on the Moon. If you wanted to script the twentieth century, you could hardly do better.

On the Boeing side, the program ran under Henry Kudish in Huntsville; at NASA, under Saverio "Sonny" Morea. Boeing built the first flight rover with roughly six hundred engineers. But the shape of the thing, the feel of it, the way it drove — that was GM.

Seventeen Months

The genuinely astonishing part isn't that it worked. It's how fast they built it.

NASA decided to proceed with the rover in late May 1969 — before Apollo 11 had even landed. It ran a competition through that summer, chose Boeing in October 1969, and demanded the first flight-ready rover at Kennedy Space Center by spring 1971.

That is seventeen months from contract to delivery. Twenty-two from the decision to build it at all.

To understand how mad that is, you need the benchmark. The Smithsonian's Apollo curators put it plainly: at an absolute minimum, Apollo hardware typically took twice as long to develop. The Command Module and the Lunar Module each took around five years, and both blew through their schedules. The rover had less than eighteen months to go from paper to a machine rated to carry two human beings on another planet.

They made it. In fact Boeing delivered the first rover on 15 March 1971 — two weeks early, which in the context of Apollo is almost suspicious.

How? Because GM had been quietly solving the hard parts for years. Its earlier "MOLAB" lunar-vehicle studies, done with Boeing, had already worked out wire-mesh wheels, the little electric motors, the harmonic-drive gearing. When the starting gun fired, they were not starting from zero. They were starting from a decade of homework nobody had asked them to finish.

This is the honest version of a claim you will sometimes see — that the LRV was "the fastest car ever developed." As a bare superlative it is hard to prove. But framed properly it is more impressive, not less: this was a full piece of crewed spacecraft, built to spacecraft standards, delivered in half the time such hardware normally demanded, and it worked flawlessly the first time it was ever switched on in the environment it was designed for. Because it could not be tested there first. There was no way to drive it in one-sixth gravity on Earth. Its maiden voyage was on the Moon.

What It Cost

The most expensive single automobile in history, and it isn't close.

The original Boeing contract was for about $19 million. By the time the first rover was delivered, cost overruns had pushed the final figure to roughly $38 million — for four vehicles. In today's money that is somewhere north of $300 million.

The U.S. Government Accountability Office, which examined the overrun, blamed a familiar cocktail: moving work between Huntsville and Seattle, aluminium welding problems in the chassis, navigation test gear, the deployment system, and GM's honest underestimation of how hard the mobility system would be — the wire wheels, the hand controller, the drive electronics.

Divide it how you like. Four flight-configured rovers, one of them ultimately cannibalised for spares, for $38 million. It remains, by a distance no other vehicle approaches, the most expensive car ever made.

And it made one horsepower.

The Tyres Are the Best Part

Woven from piano wire, shod in titanium, and completely airless — fifty years before "airless tyre" became a press-release phrase.

Rubber was a non-starter, and the reasons are a tidy summary of everything that makes the Moon horrible. There is no air, so a pneumatic tyre has nothing to hold. Temperatures swing hundreds of degrees between sunlight and shadow. The dust is abrasive and gets into everything. And there is no possible way to fix a puncture two hundred and forty thousand miles from a garage.

So Pavlics's team built something entirely different. Each wheel was:

A spun aluminium hub, wrapped in a tyre 32 inches across and 9 inches wide, woven from zinc-coated steel piano wire — strands about three-hundredths of an inch thick. Titanium chevrons riveted across roughly half the contact area gave it bite in the dust. Inside sat a smaller titanium "bump stop" frame, so that if the wire tyre deformed too far over a rock, it met the frame instead of crushing the hub. The whole assembly weighed about twelve pounds.

Think of it as a springy metal basket. It could deform like a tyre without needing air, spread the vehicle's weight across soft regolith, bite for traction, and shrug off rocks that would have destroyed a rigid wheel. It was designed to survive at least 112 miles of driving — more than double what all three rovers combined would ever actually cover.

Four Motors, One Horsepower, Enormous Cleverness

Each wheel had its own electric motor producing a quarter of one horsepower — spinning at 10,000 rpm and geared down through an 80:1 harmonic drive to turn that frantic little whirr into slow, deliberate, high-torque wheel motion. Four of them. One horsepower, total, to move two astronauts and their equipment.

On Earth that would be a joke. On the Moon, where everything weighs a sixth of what it does here, it was plenty — because the rover was geared for torque and patience, not speed.

The drive units were sealed and pressurised with nitrogen, because lunar dust is a mechanical assassin: it destroys seals, bearings and radiators. And the whole thing was built with the Apollo obsession for redundancy. If a wheel motor died, the astronauts could pull a pin, disengage that wheel, and let it freewheel while the other three carried on. Analysis suggested the rover could limp along on a single working drive if it truly had to.

No Steering Wheel

A conventional wheel was impossible — a man in a pressurised Apollo suit simply cannot move his arms and wrists that way. So the rover was steered with a T-shaped hand controller mounted between the two seats. Push forward to go. Pull back to slow and stop. Lean it left or right to steer. Haul it all the way back for the parking brake; flick a switch and pull back to reverse. Either astronaut could drive, though the commander, in the left seat, usually did.

Better still, it steered at both ends. Front and rear wheels both turned, giving it a turning circle roughly its own length — which is why, in the footage, it looks so improbably nimble, crabbing and pivoting through terrain that would have beached an ordinary car. And, this being Apollo, if one steering system failed the other could carry the whole job alone.

Apollo Lunar Roving Vehicle — the vital numbers
Official nameApollo Lunar Roving Vehicle (LRV)
NicknameMoon Buggy — a play on 'dune buggy'
Prime contractorBoeing (Seattle / Huntsville)
Mobility subcontractorGM Defense Research Laboratories / Delco, Santa Barbara
Managed byNASA Marshall Space Flight Center
BuiltFour. Apollo 15, 16, 17 — plus one cannibalised for spares.
CrewTwo astronauts in pressurised suits
Length10 ft 2 in
Wheelbase7.5 ft
Height44.8 in
Earth weight≈462 lb empty (about 77 lb on the Moon)
Payload≈1,080 lb — more than twice its own weight
MotorsFour brushed DC motors, one per wheel
Power0.25 hp each · about 1 hp total
Gearing80:1 harmonic drive, sealed and nitrogen-pressurised
BatteriesTwo 36-volt silver-zinc · non-rechargeable
Wheels32 in diameter, 9 in wide, ≈12 lb each
TiresWoven zinc-coated steel piano wire, titanium chevron treads
SteeringFront and rear, via a T-bar hand controller — no wheel
Top design speed≈8 mph
Fastest recorded11.2 mph — Cernan's unofficial lunar land-speed record
NavigationDead reckoning — gyro, odometers, onboard computer
Total distance driven57.5 miles across three missions
Still on the MoonYes. All three. Forever, more or less.

What It Was Actually Made Of

A car is its materials. This one was an anthology of them — most exotic, some astonishingly humble.

Start with the skeleton. The chassis was welded tubing in aluminium alloy 2219 — a specific, deliberate choice, not just "aluminium." 2219 is a copper-bearing alloy prized for holding its strength across ferocious temperature swings and for welding cleanly, which matters enormously when your frame is a lattice of welded joints that will bake in raw sunlight and freeze in shadow within the same hour. The three-part frame was hinged so it could fold, and here is a detail that tells you how ruthless the weight-saving was: engineers shaved metal from the tube walls in the low-stress areas, thinning the structure anywhere the loads allowed it. Even so, the finished rover came in around sixty pounds over its 400-pound target — a miss that reportedly cost the team its weight-reduction bonus.

The wheels were that woven zinc-coated steel piano wire with titanium chevrons riveted across the tread and a titanium bump-stop frame within. The Smithsonian, which holds a flight-spare wheel, lists its materials with beautiful economy: Zinc coated steel, titanium. Nothing else. A tyre made of two metals and the absence of air.

The fenders — the parts that broke, the parts that mattered so much for dust — were epoxy-impregnated fibreglass. The seats were tubular aluminium strung with nylon webbing, like the world's most expensive lawn chairs, over aluminium floor panels, with Velcro seatbelts. There was brass in the fittings. There was canvas. The Smithsonian's summary of a qualification unit reads almost like a poem when you line it up: Aluminum chassis, zinc-coated piano wire wheels, fiberglass fenders, canvas, nylon, brass.

Aluminium, titanium, steel, fibreglass, nylon, canvas, brass — and Velcro. The most expensive vehicle ever built was held together, in part, with the same hook-and-loop strip that fastens a child's shoes.

That is the thing people miss about Apollo hardware. It was, at once, the most advanced engineering on Earth and a scrapyard of ordinary materials used with extraordinary discipline. There was no room for anything heavier than it needed to be, and no romance about exotic substances for their own sake. If nylon webbing would do the job of a seat, the seat was nylon webbing. If a household fastener would hold a seatbelt shut through a Moon mission, it did.

The Suspension Nobody Talks About

Under all of it sat a genuine, sophisticated suspension — not a rigid axle, not a compromise, but a proper double-wishbone setup at each corner. Each wheel hung on upper and lower A-arms, sprung by torsion bars, with a damper between the chassis and the upper wishbone. Four-wheel independent suspension, in other words, on a car built to be used for three days and then left where it stopped.

Why bother? Because the lunar surface is worse than it looks, and because one-sixth gravity does strange things to a vehicle. With so little weight holding it down, the rover wanted to bound and skip over every ripple; without real suspension travel and damping, a wheel that left the ground — and they frequently did, all four at once in the Grand Prix footage — would come back down uncontrolled. The wishbones and torsion bars gave each wheel the travel to follow the terrain, and the dampers kept the whole thing from becoming a pogo stick. It is the least visible and most underrated part of the entire design, and it is the reason the rover could be driven hard rather than merely crept along.

How You Fold a Car Into a Spaceship

Ten feet of vehicle, into a wedge the size of a large car's boot.

This may be the most quietly brilliant piece of engineering in the whole story, and it exists because of a scale model and a child's toy.

By May 1969 the Apollo programme was already far advanced, and nobody had left much room for a ten-foot car. The only space remaining in the Lunar Module was a wedge-shaped bay about five feet on a side — Quadrant 1 — little bigger than the boot of a large saloon. The rover had to fit in there, and then unfold itself onto the surface with a single astronaut pulling on ropes.

Pavlics's answer was a car that folded in on itself: the front and rear chassis sections hinged over the centre, the wheels tucking flat against the body. To sell the idea to NASA, Sam Romano built a scale model to prove it would fit and deploy. His wife sewed the little fabric seats. And his seven-year-old son donated a GI Joe — the American cousin of Action Man — to sit in it and demonstrate the scale.

The vehicle that carried humans across the Moon was pitched to NASA with a doll borrowed from a seven-year-old.

The real deployment was a piece of theatre in itself. One astronaut climbed partway down the Lunar Module's ladder and released the rover; the other, on the surface, drew it out on a system of pulleys, braked reels, ropes and cloth tapes. As it lowered, most of it happened automatically — the rear wheels swung out and locked, then the front, and the whole frame settled onto the dust. The system was designed to work even if the lander sat tilted on a slope, at any of several heights above the surface. Then the astronauts raised the seats and footrests, folded out the fenders, powered it up, and backed their car away from their spaceship.

Finding the Way Home

There were no roads, no landmarks a newcomer could trust, and — going away from or into the low sun — a disorienting landscape where you often couldn't see a crater until you were on top of it. So the rover carried a dead-reckoning navigation system: a directional gyroscope, odometers counting wheel turns, and a small computer that continuously worked out the vehicle's heading, the distance travelled, and — the number that actually mattered — the straight-line direction and distance back to the Lunar Module.

Could they just follow their own tracks back? Sometimes. But NASA was never going to let two lives depend on tyre marks in the dust. Across all three missions, over every mile driven, the little navigation system never once failed.

The Drives Themselves

Three rovers. Three missions. And, this being human beings let loose with a car in a place no one had ever driven, a great deal of incident.

Apollo 15: The Steering Fixed Itself Overnight

The first Moon drive nearly started with a dead car. Scott and Irwin unfolded the rover, climbed aboard — and discovered the front steering didn't work. Somewhere in Mission Control, a room full of GM engineers presumably stopped breathing.

But the rover steered at both ends. NASA let them press on using only the rear wheels. And the next day, the front steering mysteriously started working. Scott joked to Houston that maybe the Marshall Space Flight Center engineers had flown up to the Moon overnight and fixed it. A normal car with dead front steering is finished. This one just went exploring on the other end.

Then there is the Seatbelt Basalt. Driving back to the lander, Scott spotted a rock he wanted — a rock that was not on the tightly-scheduled stop list. So he announced he needed to fiddle with his troublesome seatbelt, stopped, and used the moment to grab the sample. It turned out to be a vesicular olivine basalt, and the Smithsonian has it to this day, catalogued forever under the little white lie that captured it.

The ride, by the way, was not smooth. The ground looked gentle from a distance and was, up close, a mess of small craters and ripples and deceptive shadows. Irwin compared the experience to riding a bucking bronco, with at least one wheel frequently off the ground entirely. This is what the seatbelts were for.

Apollo 16: The Lunar Grand Prix

John Young — commander, and by all accounts a man who enjoyed himself — put the Apollo 16 rover through what NASA openly called the "Grand Prix," a flat-out handling run while Charlie Duke filmed it on a 16mm camera. Part of it was genuine engineering evaluation: NASA wanted to see the suspension, the wheels, the dust behaviour, the stability at speed. The rest of it looks exactly like what it was — a man given the first car on another world, briefly seeing what it would do.

Apollo 16 had its own steering scare, too. The rear steering was dead during the first drive, later traced to an open circuit near the hand controller. And again, front-and-rear steering turned a potential mission-ender into a shrug. That redundancy wasn't a luxury feature. It was insurance, and it paid out twice.

Apollo 17: The Fender, Twice

The greatest field repair in the history of the automobile, and it took two tries.

Lunar dust is genuinely dangerous. Without a fender, a spinning wire wheel throws a great arcing "rooster tail" of the stuff over everything — suits, instruments, and critically the thermal-control surfaces that keep the rover from cooking itself. Dark dust absorbs heat. Dust on a radiator is a slow death.

On Apollo 17's first drive, a hammer in the shin pocket of Gene Cernan's suit caught the right-rear fender extension and tore it off. Oh — there goes a fender, Cernan can be heard saying, with the particular weariness of a man who has just created a problem. He taped it back on. But — and here is the detail the tidy version leaves out — good old American grey tape does not stick to lunar-dust-covered anything, and after about an hour the fender fell off again. Schmitt noticed only because he was suddenly being, in his words, rained on with dust.

So Houston went to work overnight while the astronauts slept. Engineers devised a replacement from four laminated pages of lunar surface maps, taped together and clamped to the rail where the fender had been — and John Young test-fitted the whole fix on a mock-up rover back on Earth before they talked Cernan and Schmitt through building it. This time it held. The dust problem dropped to something manageable, and the crew got another fifteen hours of driving out of it.

Forget baling wire and a roadside Jeep. They fixed a car on the Moon with four map pages and tape — after the first repair fell off.

Before they left, they pulled all four fenders — the three survivors and the map-and-tape replacement — off the rover to bring home. That improvised fender, lunar dust still ground into it, is in the Smithsonian's National Air and Space Museum today.

And the rover earned its keep in the most literal way. It was at Shorty Crater, having been driven there, that geologist-astronaut Harrison Schmitt suddenly called out that there was orange soil — genuine surprise in his voice. It turned out to be tiny beads of titanium-rich volcanic glass, evidence of ancient fire fountains on a world everyone assumed had always been dead. Without the rover to reach Shorty, that discovery almost certainly never happens.

The Last Thing It Did

At the end of Apollo 17 — the end of the entire Apollo era, though nobody fully admitted it yet — Cernan parked the rover a distance from the Lunar Module and pointed its television camera back at the lander.

The camera was operated from Earth by a NASA controller named Ed Fendell, nicknamed "Captain Video." Because radio takes about a second and a half to cross from Earth to the Moon, Fendell had to send the command to tilt the camera up before the Lunar Module actually lifted off, timing his blind guess so the camera would be looking in the right place when the ascent stage fired. Twice before, on earlier missions, he'd missed. On Apollo 17, the third and final attempt, he got it — and the camera caught the ascent stage rising off the surface in a spray of gold foil, climbing into a black sky.

Then the picture held still. The last two men to walk on the Moon were gone, and their car sat alone in the silence, watching them leave. It is still there. It never stopped pointing at the spot.

The Buggy That Sold Colour Televisions

Here is a thing people forget: by Apollo 14, America was getting bored of the Moon. Another landing, another two men in white walking carefully around. Ratings sagged.

The rover fixed that, too. Apollo 15 suddenly looked like an adventure — mountains, a canyon-like rille, a moving camera, and a car. Newspapers and television found it irresistible. Advertisers rode the wave; "Moon Buggy" fever was used to sell colour TV sets. The most advanced electric vehicle ever built doubled, briefly, as the twentieth century's greatest piece of automotive marketing.

But They Weren't First

The first wheels on the Moon were Soviet, robotic, and eight months early.

For all the American iconography, the honest record is this: the first vehicle to roll across the lunar surface was the Soviet Union's Lunokhod 1. It rolled down a ramp off the Luna 17 lander on 17 November 1970 — roughly eight months before Apollo 15 — and over the following ten months this eight-wheeled, bathtub-shaped robot drove more than ten kilometres, controlled by a team back on Earth, solar-powered by day and kept from freezing at night by a little nuclear heater.

It carried no people, which is the distinction that matters and the reason the Americans still claim the more romantic milestone. Lunokhod put the first wheels on the Moon. The LRV put the first drivers there. Both are true, and the Soviet machine deserves far better than the footnote it usually gets.

The Sequel · Fifty Years Later

The Moon Buggy Is Coming Back

And this time it is not one NASA car. It is a fleet, built by companies, rented by the government.

The spiritual successor to the LRV has a duller name — the Lunar Terrain Vehicle, or LTV — and a completely different philosophy. NASA program managers describe it as a mix between the Apollo Lunar Roving Vehicle and a Mars-style rover: astronauts can drive it, but it can also trundle around on its own, or under remote control from Earth, in the long stretches between crewed visits. And NASA isn't buying the rover outright the way it bought the LRV. It is renting lunar mobility as a service — the companies build, own and operate the vehicles, and NASA pays for the miles. The overall Lunar Terrain Vehicle Services contract carries a ceiling of $4.6 billion through 2039.

In May 2026, at an event NASA called Moon Base, the agency named its first two winners — and pointedly chose two, not one. NASA administrator Jared Isaacman argued that building a single exquisite rover does nothing to stimulate a lunar economy; he wanted multiples of everything. So both finalists got funded:

Astrolab CLV-1

Astrolab took the roughly $219 million award for its Crewed Lunar Vehicle 1, adapted from the company's larger FLEX rover. It's the futuristic-looking one: around 4 metres long deployed, a maximum mass near 2,000 pounds — roughly four times an LRV — and a top speed of about 6 mph, which is almost exactly Apollo's. The difference isn't pace. It's persistence: this thing is built to keep working for a year, not three days.

Its team includes Axiom Space, Interlune and Odyssey Space Research, with wheel, battery and battery-management technology from Venturi Space. And the wheels are the direct heirs to Pavlics's piano-wire idea — not rubber, but modern non-pneumatic "hyper-deformable" wheels designed to flex over rocks and survive the radiation and cold. Fifty years on, the answer to the lunar tyre problem is still: no air.

Lunar Outpost Pegasus

The one where General Motors and Goodyear both come back to the Moon.

The other roughly $220 million award went to Lunar Outpost's Pegasus — and it closes the circle beautifully, because the team behind it is Lunar Outpost with General Motors, Goodyear and Leidos. The same two names, GM and Goodyear, that helped build and shoe the original.

Pegasus is designed to run for about a year, drive itself or be teleoperated or carry crew, and it deliberately draws on Apollo heritage. NASA lists its speed at more than 9 mph. GM's role this time is centred on adapting its production electric-vehicle battery technology to survive the brutal thermal swings of the lunar south pole; Goodyear is once again developing airless lunar tyres, because — say it with me — rubber still won't work up there.

One note on range, since the older figures floating around are out of date. Earlier proposals for Lunar Outpost's larger Eagle rover talked about 900-kilometre ranges. But after a NASA reset in early 2026 that asked for simpler, cheaper, faster vehicles, the LTVs actually bought are specced closer to a 200-kilometre range and about 10 km/h. That is still roughly five times the LRV's entire cumulative design distance — in a single vehicle that then goes back to work.

The two rovers will be delivered to the surface not by their makers but by Blue Origin — Jeff Bezos's company, and yes, the one occasionally misremembered as "Blue Horizon" — whose Blue Moon Mark 1 lander won the delivery contract. Blue Origin's main Artemis job is its human lander; here it's the moving truck.

Notably absent from the winners was Intuitive Machines, whose Moon RACER design — with an airless Michelin wheel — had been one of three finalists in the earlier 2024 round. Its share price, which had climbed in anticipation, fell sharply when NASA read out two names that weren't its own.

Then and now
APOLLO LRVARTEMIS LTV (new)
Era1971–72Planned from 2028
OwnershipNASA hardwareCommercial service — NASA rents it
OperationManual onlyCrewed, teleoperated, or autonomous
Mission lifeA few days, then abandonedUp to a year, left working between crews
WhereEquatorial Apollo sitesThe lunar south pole
Range57.5 mi total, all missions~200 km design range each
Top speed8 mph design~6 mph (10 km/h)
TiresWoven piano wire + titaniumModern airless / non-pneumatic
After the missionLeft behindReused as lunar infrastructure

And the Lunar Motorhome

Toyota and Japan are building something you can live inside.

Running alongside all of this is a completely different kind of machine, and it is not a buggy at all. Since 2019, Toyota and the Japanese space agency JAXA have been developing a pressurised rover nicknamed the Lunar Cruiser — a name chosen, Toyota says, because "Land Cruiser" has always meant surviving harsh places.

It is roughly the size of two microbuses — about 6 metres long — runs on fuel cells, and lets two astronauts live and work inside, in shirtsleeves, without suits, for up to about thirty days. Under a 2024 agreement, Japan designs, builds and operates it; NASA launches and delivers it; and in exchange, two Japanese astronauts get seats to the lunar surface on future Artemis missions.

So the family tree now runs like this. The Apollo LRV was a dune buggy. The new Astrolab and Lunar Outpost machines are lunar UTVs — work trucks. And the Toyota Lunar Cruiser is the expedition camper: not fast, but built to go far and keep you alive inside it.

What Actually Changed

The best way to understand the leap is to stop thinking about speed, because nobody is trying to go fast on the Moon. One-sixth gravity makes a vehicle skip and float and brake strangely; the limit was never horsepower, it was traction and nerve.

What changed is ambition. The LRV was an accessory to a visit: unfold it, drive it for three days, leave it. The new machines are meant to be permanent infrastructure — delivered ahead of the crew, driven by astronauts when they're there, driven by Earth when they're not, hauling cargo, scouting sites, surviving the two-week lunar night and the shadowed cold of the south pole, and simply staying.

If I had to put it in terms this magazine understands: the Apollo Lunar Roving Vehicle was a Lotus Seven for the Moon — brutally light, gloriously minimal, purpose-built, and over almost as soon as it began. The new rovers are lunar Land Cruisers. Less romantic. Built to still be running when you need them next year.

Three Cars, Still Parked

Everything else came home, or tried to. The astronauts rode the ascent stage back to lunar orbit; the descent stages, the experiments, the flags and the rovers stayed. There was never any plan to bring them back — every pound that returned was a pound of fuel someone had to account for, and a used Moon car did not make the list.

So they are still there. NASA's Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter has photographed the Apollo sites from above, and in the images you can still see the rover tracks and the astronauts' footpaths, scuffed into the dust and going nowhere, exactly as they were left. There is no wind to erase them, no rain, no rust, no decay — only the slow patience of micrometeorites and radiation, working on a timescale that makes human history look like an afternoon.

The tracks Apollo 17 left will likely outlast every road on Earth.

And somewhere in the Sea of Rains, and the lunar highlands at Descartes, and the valley of Taurus-Littrow, three of the most expensive and most extraordinary vehicles ever built by human hands are parked where their drivers stepped off them for the last time — hand controllers centred, one horsepower asleep, piano-wire tyres pressed into fifty-year-old dust.

Three abandoned classics, after the greatest road trip anyone has ever taken.

We really did bring a car to another world and go for a drive. There has never been anything more gloriously, absurdly, magnificently human.

Tagged: Apollo · Lunar Roving Vehicle · NASA · Boeing · General Motors · Space · Engineering · Artemis · History

Sources & Verification Core LRV specification, program history, $19M→$38M cost, four vehicles built (three flown plus one for spares), 17-month schedule, Quadrant 1 stowage, T-bar controller, dead-reckoning navigation, Cernan's 11.2 mph record: NASA NSSDC (nssdc.gsfc.nasa.gov); Wikipedia (Lunar Roving Vehicle); Saverio Morea, "The Lunar Roving Vehicle — Historical Perspective," NASA CP-3166. · Wheel construction (spun aluminium hub, zinc-coated 0.033-in woven steel wire, titanium chevrons over 50% of contact area, titanium bump-stop, ~12 lb, designed for ≥112 miles) and Delco 0.25 hp motors at 10,000 rpm through 80:1 harmonic drive: Wikipedia (LRV); NASA Glenn, "The Development of Wheels for the Lunar Roving Vehicle." · "17 months vs at least twice as long," MOLAB heritage, and delivery two weeks early: National Air and Space Museum interview with Earl Swift; Popular Science / Vintage Space; RocketSTEM (Pavlics and Romano interviews). · Wright brothers first flight 17 December 1903 (correcting 1906); mother Susan Wright died 1889; bicycle business: standard historical record. · Ferenc Pavlics — Hungarian refugee, arrived 1957, NASA recognition for the "resilient wheel"; Sam Romano's scale model, his wife's fabric seats, his son's GI Joe: Hagerty; Classic & Sports Car; NASA. · Apollo 15 front-steering fault and overnight "fix," Seatbelt Basalt, "bucking bronco": Smithsonian; Apollo Lunar Surface Journal. Apollo 16 Grand Prix and rear-steering open circuit: NASA. · Apollo 17 fender: hammer strike, failed tape repair, second failure, four-map-page replacement tested on Earth by John Young, all four fenders removed and returned, now at NASM: astronomy.com; Smithsonian; NASA. · Orange soil at Shorty Crater (titanium-rich glass beads): NASA. Ed Fendell "Captain Video" and the Apollo 17 ascent filming: Wikipedia (LRV); NASA. "Moon Buggy" selling colour TVs: Smithsonian. · Lunokhod 1 off Luna 17, 17 November 1970, >10 km: NASA; Armagh Planetarium. · Mission distances (15: ~17.5 mi; 16: 16.6 mi; 17: 22.3 mi; total 57.5 mi) and LRO imaging of tracks: NASA; GM historical summary. · Artemis LTV — May 2026 Moon Base awards (Astrolab CLV-1 ~$219M; Lunar Outpost Pegasus ~$220M, with GM, Goodyear, Leidos), $4.6B LTVS ceiling, ~200 km / ~10 km/h spec after the 2026 reset, Blue Origin Blue Moon Mark 1 delivery, Intuitive Machines not selected, Isaacman "multiples of everything": NASA; SpaceNews; SpacePolicyOnline; Payload; Lunar Outpost. · Toyota/JAXA pressurised Lunar Cruiser (since 2019, fuel-cell, ~30-day shirt-sleeve operation, 2024 NASA–Japan agreement): NASA; Toyota. · Present-day figures were verified against sources current to mid-2026 and may have moved since.
